Hepatocellular oxidant stress following intestinal ischemia-reperfusion injury (IRI) is a major concern in surgical and critical care medicine. IRI can lead to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can cause cellular damage and dysfunction. The liver is particularly susceptible to IRI-induced oxidant stress due to its high energy requirements and the presence of cytochrome P450 enzymes, which can generate ROS during the metabolism of drugs and other xenobiotics. Several studies have shown that antioxidants, such as N-acetylcysteine (NAC) and vitamin E, can attenuate hepatocellular oxidant stress and reduce liver injury following IRI. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is a serious side effect of heparin therapy that can occur in up to 5% of patients. HIT is caused by the formation of antibodies against platelet factor 4 (PF4) and platelet membrane glycoprotein IIb/IIIa (GPIIb/IIIa) complexes, which are formed when heparin binds to these proteins on the surface of platelets. The antibodies can cause platelet activation and aggregation, leading to thrombotic events such as deep vein thrombosis (DVT), pulmonary embolism (PE), and stroke. HIT can be diagnosed with a combination of clinical criteria and laboratory tests, including the heparin-induced platelet activation (HIPA) assay and the platelet immunoassay (PIA). Treatment options for HIT include discontinuation of heparin, administration of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), and platelet transfusions. The use of alternative anticoagulants, such as fondaparinux or argatroban, may also be considered in some cases.""

The basal functioning of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and psychological distress in recreational ecstasy polydrug users has been studied. The HPA axis is a complex neuroendocrine system that plays a central role in regulating the body's response to stress. The system is activated by the release of corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) from the hypothalamus, which stimulates the release of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) from the pituitary gland. ACTH, in turn, stimulates the production of cortisol and other glucocorticoids from the adrenal glands. Cortisol is a major stress hormone that plays a critical role in the body's response to stress, regulating blood sugar levels, metabolism, and immune system function. Chronic stress and dysregulation of the HPA axis have been linked to various psychopathologies, including depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).""

The mechanisms by which free radical scavengers protect against gentamicin-mediated nephropathy in rats may involve the scavenging of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the reduction of oxidative stress. ROS are generated during gentamicin treatment and contribute to the development of nephropathy by damaging cellular components, including proteins, lipids, and DNA. Free radical scavengers, such as vitamin C and E, can neutralize ROS and reduce oxidative stress, thereby protecting against gentamicin-mediated renal damage.
